title: Filtering Rows and Using subset()
description: Select rows based on conditions with logical vectors and the subset() function.

slides.title: Filtering with Logical Vectors 🔍
slides.content:
The most common way to filter rows is to provide a logical vector in the row index position:

```r
df[df$age >= 18, ]
```

You can combine conditions with `&` (AND) and `|` (OR):

```r
df[df$age >= 18 & df$gender == "F", ]
```

slides.title: Filtering Example
slides.codeSnippet:
df <- data.frame(
  name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana"),
  age = c(25, 17, 30, 16),
  gender = c("F", "M", "M", "F")
)
adults <- df[df$age >= 18, ]
print(adults)
adult_females <- df[df$age >= 18 & df$gender == "F", ]
print(adult_females)

quizData.question: What does `df[df$x > 0, ]` do?

options.text: Returns rows where column x is positive
Returns columns where x is positive
Returns a vector of positive x values

quizData.explanation: It subsets rows based on the condition, keeping all columns.

slides.title: The subset() Function
slides.content:
`subset()` is a convenient wrapper for filtering. It avoids repeating the data frame name.

```r
subset(df, age >= 18)
subset(df, age >= 18 & gender == "F", select = c(name, age))
```

The `select` argument lets you choose columns.

slides.title: subset() Example
slides.codeSnippet:
df <- data.frame(name=c("A","B","C"), age=c(20,30,25), score=c(85,90,95))
subset(df, age < 30)
subset(df, score > 90, select = name)

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: `subset()` drops rows with NA in the condition. If you need to keep NAs, use logical subsetting with `%in%` or explicit handling.
